By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate RICHMOND, B.C. - If Red Deer Rustlers find Richmond Sockeyes to be in a surly mood in tonight's opening game of their Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal series, they shouldn't be surprised. After all, the Sockeyes' general manager-coach is none other than Orland Kurtenbach, who earned a reputation as a hard-nosed, no-nonsense player during an NHL career that featured stops in New York (Rangers), Toronto and Vancouver. "Yes, I like a physical game, but I also like to see a lot of puck movement," he offered Monday during practice in preparation for tonight's 8:30 p.m. MDT contest. "What I don't like is a lot of goony stuff. There's nothing wrong with good, tough hockey as long as it doesn't take away from the flow of the game." With the Sockeyes, Kurtebach appears to have the best of both worlds. While admitting that his team is "fairly aggressive overall," he quickly pointed out that the B.C. champs are

An all-Spokane officiating crew will work the exhibition game between the U.S. and Canadian Olympic teams Nov. 28 in Tacoma. Dennis LaRue, the lone American selected to referee in the 1988 Olympics, will be the referee in the Tacoma Dome. His linesmen will be Kerry Blair and Cory Bowles. All three formerly played Junior B hockey in Spokane, and LaRue played one year of senior amateur. LaRue and Blair also will work the U.S.- Canada game Nov. 27 in Los Angeles. Mark Rudolph, officiating program director of the Amateur Hockey Association of the U.S., said LaRue may receive other exhibition assignments. Don Cherry, who played pro hockey for the 1962-63 Spokane Comets, has been inducted into the Rochester (N.Y.) Americans Hall of Fame. Cherry played and coached for Rochester in the pro American Hockey League after leaving Spokane. He later coached in the National Hockey League. Joe Crozier, another former Comet defenseman, NHL coach and Rochester coach, was one of the original inductees in the Americans' Hall of Fame last year. Dan Holden, the only local player ever to play for the Spokane Chiefs in the Western Hockey League, is playing for A Acadia University in Wolfville, Nova Scotia. Holden played at the University of Calgary in 1984-85, then joined the Chiefs the following season. Former Chief defenseman Rick Herbert, who holds the WHL record of 329 games played, has joined the Richmond Sockeyes of the Tier Two British Columbia Junior Hockey League. Marc Pezzin, who coached Herbert in Spokane and Brandon, said Herbert was not interested in playing a sixth season in the WHL after being released by the Chicago Blackhawks. Herbert plans to play college hockey in Canada next year, Pezzin said.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to an easy 7-3 win over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship yesterday. It was the second loss in two days for Dartmouth. The Nova Scotians opened the week-long, round-robin tournament Saturday with a 7-2 defeat at the hands of the host Humboldt Broncos. Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson and Matt Hervey also scored for the Sockeyes, who piled up 44 shots against Dartmouth's 29. Richmond zipped to a 3-1 lead in the first period and built the margin to 6-2 in the second. Jerry Scott had two goals and Brian King had one for the Fuel Kids, who had the bulk of the fans behind them. The Sockeyes took 11 of 17 minors and a major, and both teams had a misconduct. Humboldt was to play its second game later Sunday against the Pembroke Lumber Kings from Ontario. Richmond plays Pembroke in the only game today.

Staff Writer To longtime Clipper fans, it may be kind of funny to see Bill Hardy dressed in black and red instead of orange and white. The ex-Clipper now plays for Richmond Sockeyes, who will be at Civic Arena to face the Clippers tonight in game three of the B.C. Junior Hockey League Coastal Division best of seven semi-final series. Richmond holds a 2-0 lead consecutive wins on home ice Tuesday and Wednesday. Game three is slated for 8 p.m., and a crowd of over 1,300 is expected. Hardy, who wore Clipper colors for four years, after coming up through the Nanaimo minor hockey system, decided to play for a different team this season in hopes of securing a college scholarship. Last season, Hardy had his best season as a junior, scoring 39 goals and 72 assists, but on a team that finished below .500 and third in the standings. This season, the six-feet, 180- pounder saw his point totals drop, with 19 goals and 33 assists.Hardy, although unsuccessful in his attempt to get a scholarship so far, still feels he made the right move. "It's probably true that I would have got more points and ice time in Nanaimo, but I don't regret the move," said Hardy. "'There's still the playoffs. That's why I left Nanaimo, so I could be on a winning team.' Hardy, a top sniper with Nanaimo last season, found himself in the middle of the pack with Richmond, behind such prolific pointgetters as Dave Tomlinson, Rob Clarke and Bryan Moller. JOB HAS CHANGED He has seen, his duties slightly changed, to where he now plays more of an all-around role for the Sockeyes. "My role is to be in the corners and grind,' said Hardy. *(Linemates) Brian Kozak and Jason Phillips are very talented with the puck. I try and get them the puck from the corner and then get open." EDMONTON (CP) The Edmonton Oilers kept shaking their heads at questions Wednesday, making sure it really was May 6 and they were in the Stanley Cup semifinal and not April 9 and round one of the playoffs. "This isn't shades of LA, it's a duplicate," said coach Glen feeling a little of the deja vu himself. "I hope the next four games are the same." Sather was alluding to Edmonton losing the first game of the playoffs 5-2 to Los Angeles, then beating the Kings four straight. Edmonton swept Winnipeg before losing to the Detroit Red Wings 3-1 in Game 1 of the semifinal Tuesday. "I heard a lot of these questions after the first LA game,' Sather told reporters during the off day of the Campbell Conference final. "It was the same situation, they beat us, the next game we came back and we played like we're capable of playing." Edmonton beat Los Angeles 13-3 in Game 2 but the Oilers were unanimous in their agreement they won't likely beat the Red Wings by that lopsided a score in the second game of the best-ofseven semifinal tonight. "This team is a lot better defensively than LA, we'll have a lot tougher time beating Detroit," said Kent Nilsson, the lone Oiler scorer Tuesday. Fuel Kids finish Broncos HUMBOLDT, Sask. The losers, with a -robin record of 2-1, will meet the Pembroke Lumber Kings in tonight's Perron * 4,4 The job that currently consumes almost every waking hour for Jean Perron has gnawed at many of his predecessors, leaving a trail of frayed nerves and queasy stomachs as it spit out the victims. There was Claude Ruel, whose nervous system was so shattered, he once abandoned his spot behind the bench before a game had ended. There was Al McNeil, who couldn't survive the tumult and the shouting, even though his results were outstanding. There was Bernard Geoffrion, a fun-loving, effervescent guy who became Exhibit A in a demonstration of the effects of on-the-job stress. There was Bob Berry, who was a moderate smoker when he took the job, but sent the stocks of tobacco companies soaring by the time he relinquished it. +

paragraphs.text: cool fans Graham Parsons was thankful, although he was still wondering what all the fuss was about. The Rustler general manager appealed to Red Deer fans Monday to stop throwing debris on the ice and at the Richmond Sockeyes players, after the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association warned that if the fans didn't behave, the series would either be cancelled or moved to a neutral site. As a result, the crowd was unusually quiet for the majority of the Rustlers' 7-5 overtime win Tuesday, and well-behaved throughout the entire contest. "I asked the fans to stop throwing things, they responded in a positive fashion and I'm extremely grateful," said Parsons. "It just shows that our fans have class. "But really, there never was as much trouble as Richmond made it out to be. Everything was blown out of proportion the last two games." Parsons wasn't impressed with the CAHA's get-tough approach. "They said we had three options - the series could be awarded to Richmond, it could be moved, or it could be cancelled. "Fifty per cent of the blame has to fall on Richmond, yet they're getting all the breaks (from the CAHA). "All the life has been taken out of the series." Parsons urged fans to bring signs and banners to tonight's game.

By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate With close to 1,700 fans roaring at the top of their lungs, Richmond Sockeyes' forward Jason Phillips must have felt the heat. Certainly, Red Deer Rustler goaltender Ken Weiss did. Fortunately for Weiss and the Rustlers, Phillips' thermostat was turned up little higher. The Sockeyes' ace sniper came up empty in a one-on-one duel with Weiss late in a 10- minute overtime period Tuesday at the Arena. Rustlers held on to their one-goal cushion, added an empty net marker 14 seconds later and scored a 7-5 win, taking a 3-2 lead in the best-seven Western Centennial Cup semifinal series. The sixth game is tonight at 7:30 p.m. at the Arena. With 24 seconds left in the overtime stanza, Richmond was awarded a penalty shot after referee Cliff Stauble ruled a Red Deer player had intentionally dislodged the Rustler goal. While Phillips stood at center ice, sizing up the opportunity, Rustler winger Rich Anderson persuaded the crowd to turn up the volume. Phillips skated in on Weiss and shot low to the corner, but instead of finding the mesh, it was blocked by the goaltender's pad. "I thought he was going to try to deke me," said a relieved Weiss, who turned in his fourth straight glittering performance, stopping 46 shots. "But he kept holding the puck to the outside. My heart was pounding away, I went into a butterfly and it hit me. "I felt confident I could stop it, but it was a lot tougher for him than it was for me. . If he makes it, they're still alive. If he doesn't, he's the goat." The first two periods of the game had all the intensity of a taffy-pull. In the most lacklustre contest of the series to date, Rustlers led 3-2 after one period and 4-3 after two, before being outscored 2-1 in the third. Dixon Ward notched the winner just 59 seconds into overtime and the Rustlers moved to within one win of a date with Humboldt Broncos in the Western final, and an automatic berth in the Centennial Cup (Canadian championship) slated for next month in Humboldt. The best-of-seven Western final starts Monday in Humboldt. "We sort of moonwalked through the first two periods," said Rustler coach Larry Billows. "We had more life in the third, but maybe that's because the fans finally started to get excited." With •Big Brother' the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association monitoring the situation. the Red Deer crowd was less lively Tuesday. The CAHA warned that the series would be either cancelled or moved to a neutral location if the fans didn't stop harassing the Sockeyes and throwing debris into their bench. "That's the way hockey should be played (without fan interference)." noted Sockeye general manager-coach Orland Kurtenbach. •What bothered me the first two games here (Saturday and Sunday) was not just the fans, but the refereeing. ATLANTIC CITY, N..J./ The Canadian Press Michael Olajide is ready to take on Thomas Hearns for the International Boxing Federation middleweight crown, but the trainer of his latest victim says the Canadian got lucky. "Michael Olajide got a Mothers Day gift," said Angelo Dundee after his fighter, Troy Darrell, lost to Olajide yesterday in a unanimous, 10-round decision. Olajide, of Vancouver, 23-0 and ranked No. 2 by the IBF, admitted the fight wasn't his best, and Dundee was quick to agree. "I'm amazed my guy lost," said Dundee. "Olajide definitely got licked." Judge Al De Vito scored it 96-92 for Olajide, while Rocky Castellani's scorecard was 97-94 and William Kostrub's read 95-94. The victory means Olajide will be negotiating today at IBF headquarters in Newark with Hearns for a battle for the vacant middleweight title. Olajide will fight either Hearns, the No. 1 contender, or the next leading available contender. Olajide, who has moved to New York City to train, floored Darrell twice in the opening round with quick flurries to the head but couldn't finish the job. Darrell, from New Rochelle, N.Y., bolstered by surviving the round, then waged an inspired inside fight. By ELLIOTT PAP Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, Sask. - It was a bedraggled, dishevelled •but euphoric group of Richmond Sockeyes who pulled out of this prairie town Sunday, their mission accomplished. Stowed among their gear and luggage, in an unassuming crate marked 'fragile', stood the symbol of their achievement. The Sockwere bringing home the Centennial Cup. Coach Orland Kurtenbach's troops stunned the cocky Humboldt Broncos 5-2 Saturday night to win the tier two junior hockey championship of Canada. The triumph capped? a remarkable post-season for the Sockeyes, who won 27 of 34 playoff games and five different titles: B.C. Junior League, Mowat Cup, AlbertaB.C., Abbott Cup (western Canada) and the Centennial Cup. "It's just an A-1 feeling," beamed Kurtenbach, a winner in his first crack at coaching junior. "I don't care if it's the Stanley Cup, Memorial Cup, Adams Cup (Central League) or Centennial Cup, The it's total elation." by Russ Ullyot The Chilliwack Progress The British Columbia. Junior Hockey League begins its 1987-88 season this weekend, with the experts watching to see if Richmond Sockeyes can repeat their powerhouse | performance of last spring. After winning the Coastal Division, the Sockeyes went on the Centennial Cup, emblamatic of Tier Two Junior A hockey supremacy in Canada. The Sockeyes didn't have the best regular season record in the BCJHL last year = that distinction belonged to the Kelowna Packers, who lost only five of of 52 games all season. The BCJHL has added a 12th team for 1 the '87-88 season. Back in the fold after a one-year leave are the Salmon Arm Tigers, who will be part of the Interior Division, which includes Kelowna Packers, Vernon Lakers, Penticton Knights, Merritt Centennials and Summerland Buckaroos. The Coast Division features Richmond Sockeyes, Delta Flyers, Nanaimo Clippers, Juan De Fuca Whalers, Abbotsford Falcons and Chilliwack Eagles. (Here is a preview of the Falcon's Coastal Division competition.) Richmond Sockeyes Last year's record: 38-14-0 - first place. The Sockeyes have just four players - none of whom play defence - returning from last year's championship roster, but coach Orland Kurtenbach knows his players and the organization does one of the best jobs of recruiting in all of hockey. Last year's club also had only four returning players, and two those were traded away by Christmas. The only player who has been with the club for more than one season is 20-year-old goalie Glenn Pencer, who is entering his fifth season with the Sockeyes. Last year, he posted a 4.20 goals-against average, while sharing the netminding with Frank Romeo. The other returning players include right winger Rob Clarke, 20. Clarke was third in the Coast Division scoring with 65 goals and 35 assists last year. Centre Jason Tallo, 17, and right winger Tony Bobbit, 19, round out the four returnees.

Something's rotten at the Red Deer Arena and it's not dead fish! The stink is coming from a small group of people who think that by paying their $6 or $4 admission, they can do anything short of pulling a shotgun. Those kind of people can't even be called fans. It's them who are throwing coins, empty aluminum cans, golfballs, and other garbage, most of which has been directed at the B.C. champion Richmond Sockeyes. The clowns throwing debris could force the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association to cancel the series or move it elsewhere. The culprits are not all adults or teenagers either. Many of them are youngsters who have been seen tossing aluminum cans onto the ice after every Red Deer goal. Beach balls aren't dangerous. Dead fish aren't safe either, but even National Hockey League crowds toss chickens and the odd octopus onto the ice. Coins on the ice can cause a player to trip and become seriously injured. Flying aluminum cans, eggs and golfballs can blind an eye or cause other injuries. Tossing cans at players involved in a scuffle could hit anyone, including linesmen or the referee. An errant toss could even fall short and hit someone in the crowd. Six dollars doesn't entitle anyone to throw objects onto the playing surface or at the other team. Six dollars allows you to holler for your team, clap and even get involved in a wave or two. Exercise your lungs and boo. Jeer the referee if you want. For a lousy $6, I certainly wouldn't want to live with the fact a tossed coin, can or golfball hurt someone. There are those who would argue they're only getting even for the treatment endured by Red Deer when they played last week in B.C. Richmond has already paid that price with a $500 fine for lack of security. Eggs and tomatoes flew at Red Deer players and there's even a report one Richmond fan was seen with rocks. Two wrongs don't make a right and could result in a serious injury. The behavior of the two teams doesn't help. I've watched junior hockey for about a dozen years now and Richmond is perhaps the most undisciplined team I've seen. As one Western Hockey League scout said Saturday when you have a junior club loaded with 20-year-olds you can go one of two ways. The older players will either march over the opposition with talent and experience or they will lose their composure and run amuck. Richmond has 10 players on its roster aged 20. Richmond, at least in the last two games, has attempted to intimidate Red Deer and it hasn't, and won't, work. The only thing its done is offer Red Deer a chance to work its powerplay. The two games here produced a ghastly 655 minutes in penalties, 360 to Richmond. Granted, a large portion of the time 300 minutes - is the obligatory 10-minute game misconduct for fighting. However, there's still another 355 minutes of fighting,

By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate His numbers told the story Ken Weiss was the Alberta Junior Hockey League's best goaltender this season. He's now in the process of proving statistics don't lie. The 21-year-old native of Englewood, Colorado, backstopped Sherwood Park Crusaders to the North Division pennant, led them past Fort Saskatchewan Traders in a semifinal round, but couldn't prevent St. Albert Saints from winning a tough, seven-game North final. His season didn't end there, however. South Division winner Red Deer Rustlers ousted the Saints in six games in the AJHL championship series, named Weiss as their special affiliate, and headed west to take on B.C. champion Richmond Sockeyes. Rustler regular Glen Furgason opened the series, was the unfortunate victim of an 11-4 bombing, and Weiss has been in the Red Deer net ever since. With the Crusaders' puckstopper minding the store, the Rustlers fell 6-4 in Richmond, but have since rebounded to deadlock the best-of-seven series at 2-2 with 6-3 and 7-1 wins in Red Deer Saturday and Sunday. By GREG MEACHEM of The Advocate RICHMOND, B.C. Richmond Sockeyes are named after one of British Columbia's most famous natural resources - the Sockeye salmon, a spirited, but hardly voracious breed of fish. In Tuesday's opening game of the Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal at the Richmond Arena, they looked more like sharks. Or barracudas. Or piranhas. Or all three. Sockeyes were hungry, it was feeding time, and the Red Deer Rustlers just happened to be on the menu. Rustlers meekly allowed themselves to be devoured 11-4. The second game of the bestof-seven series will be played here tonight at 8:30 p.m. MDT, with the remainder to be played in Red Deer. The ridiculously easy win, witnessed by about 1,250 fans, was the Sockeys' 16th without a loss in post-season play. And, at no time Tuesday were they in serious trouble of marring their impressive record. "I was very pleased with the effort of our team tonight," said Richmond general managercoach Orland Kurtenbach. "Hard work is our key to success, and we haven't been outworked too often in the playoffs." Certainly, they weren't outhustled by the Rustlers, who never fully recovered from starry Sockeye forward Dave Tomlinson's opening goal just 21 seconds into the contest. "Our defence was awful, I've never see Furgie (goaltender Glen Furgason) look so bad, and less than half of our forwards came to play," snapped Rustler coach Larry Billows. "Richmond worked hard, beat us to the puck, and generally just played the game the way it's supposed to be played. Meanwhile, we didn't have any intensity or desire at all." Richmond, faster, bigger and more talented than any team the Rustlers have faced this season, fired 60 shots at Furgason, who couldn't be blamed for the vast majority of the goals. Red Deer, in comparison, directed 34 at Sockeyes netminder Frank Romeo, who wasn't required to make more than a half dozen quality saves. Just over a minute after Tomlinson opened the scoring. standout defenceman Matt Hervey doubled the count with a blast from the point. But the Rustlers, on the strength of power play goals from Kevan Melrose and Greg Pulliam, were able to get out of the period with a 4-2 deficit. Sun News Services RED DEER, Alta. - Red Deer Rustlers beat the Richmond Sockeyes 7-5 in overtime Tuesday in a Centennial Cup playoff hockey game that saw no repeat of the unruly fan behavior that marred Sunday's match. The victory enabled the Rustlers to take a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven tier two junior western semi-final series. The sixth game goes here tonight. "There wasn't a bit of trouble," said Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach. "The management asked the fans to refrain from throwing anything on the ice and there was no problem. Dixon Ward's goal 59 seconds into the 10-minute overtime period was the winner. Geoff Gosselin added an insurance marker at 9:50 into an empty net just after Sockeye Jason Phillips missed a penalty shot. The Sockyes were led by Brian Kozak, who had two goals, while Paul Rutherford, Mike Claringbull and Phillips contributed singles. The Rustlers held period leads of 3-2 and 4-3, fell behind 5-4 in the third period but managed to pull even at 13:16 to force the overtime. Richard Anderson and Brian Puhalsky each scored twice for Red Deer and Greg Pulliam added a single.

Flyers 5, Islanders 1 PHILADELPHIA (AP) - The Philadelphia Flyers may have won the battle and lost the war. The Flyers erupted for three firstperiod goals Saturday night to beat the New York Islanders 5-1, winning their NHL Patrick Division final series in seven games and advancing to the Stanley Cup semifinals against the Montreal Canadiens. The Canadiens reached the Wales Conference final with a 5-3 victory Saturday over the Quebec Nordiques, also in a seventh game. When the Flyers take the ice here tonight for the first game of the bestof-seven series, the injury-riddled division champs might best be called survivors. Coach Mike Keenan's best scorer, Tim Kerr, is listed as day-to-day with a dislocated shoulder. And captain Dave Poulin has a broken rib and played Saturday night loaded with pain-killer, seeing no action after the first period. "He was in pain every minute he was out there," defenceman Mark Howe said of Poulin. "But we needed him. He gave all he could give." Aches And Pains J. J. Daigneault is nursing a tender ankle, Ilkka Sinisalo has sore stomach muscles, Ron Sutter is getting over a 2½-month layoff with a back injury, and Murray Craven is gone with a broken foot. In Saturday's deciding game, a fast start by the Flyers spelled a quick end for the Islanders' playoff hopes. Brian . Propp and Brad Marsh scored short-handed goals 44 seconds apart midway through the first period as the Flyers raced to a 3-0 lead. Dave Brown scored at 6:21 before Propp and Marsh both scored with Lindsay Carson in the penalty box. The two short-handed goals in one period matched a Stanley Cup record shared by 16 other teams. By JACK WILSON of The Advocate The Alberta-B.C. junior hockey championship could be cancelled or moved unless fans behave, the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association says. The association said in an announcement today that unruly behavior of fans at the Arena has cast a shadow over the series between the Red Deer Rustlers and the Richmond Sockeyes.

He was arrested and released following the game which was won 7-1 by Red Deer to even the best-of-seven series at two games each. Sgt. Williams said three policemen were at the Arena. He said the policemen were not assigned to the game but patrolled the building as part of their normal patrol zone. Arena manager Albert Mason said his staff didn't call the police to help quell the secondperiod disturbance. The Rustlers have between 10-12 security members. The game was delayed 30 minutes by four on-ice battles, as well as a fracas behind the Richmond Sockeyes players bench that was instigated by a group of disorderly fans who threw debris at the Richmond players, including eggs. Red Deer fans have thrown debris after every Rustler goal, including eggs, tomatoes, and fish. One player on the Richmond bench was hit in the face by an egg. The series has been punctuated by fan disturbances. Last week in Richmond two Red Deer players recieved suspensions of one and three games each for a post-game altercation with fans. The Richmond club was fined $500 by the CAHA for failing to provide proper security at that game.

NBA awards franchises NEW YORK (AP) - Charlotte, N.C., Minneapolis, Orlando, Fla., and Miami were awarded expansion franchises on Wednesday by the National Basketball Association board of governors after a four-hour meeting., Charlotte and Miami will be admitted for the 1988-89 season. Minneapolis and Orlando enter the following year. Each expansion franchise will pay an entry fee of $32.5million US. Bids from Toronto and Orange County, Calif., were rejected. The Toronto bid was spearheaded by businessman David Fingold. The other principals were Bill Ballard, son of Toronto Maple Leafs owner Harold Ballard; Dusty Cohl; Michael Cohl; Robert Cohl and former NBA great Wilt Chamberlain. The Toronto group had planned to use Maple Leaf Gardens as the team's home base.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - Barry Kress set up four goals as the Richmond Sockeyes outhustled the Delta Flyers to record a 7-2 British Columbia Junior Hockey League victory Wednesday night. Rob Clarke and Dwayne Wills each scored two goals for Richmond while goaltender Willie Popp stopped 35 shots. Chris Dixon made only 16 saves while allowing seven goals for the losers. The win gives the Sockeyes sole possession of second place in the Coastal Division, 11 points behind the first -place Flyers.

Quesnel Millionaires have gone back to life in the gutter. By dumping Grande Prairie North Stars 4-1 in the best-of-seven championship series, the Mills moved into the aristocracy of the Peace Cariboo Junior Hockey League. They were considered the favorites after eliminating the four-time PCJHL champion Prince George Spruce Kings in the semifinals and proved their worth by soundly defeating the Stars in the final. But, now, the Millionaires have to go against an even bigger fish - the Richmond Sockeyes. Tomorrow night, the Millionaires begin a best-ofthree series in Richmond to determine the B.C. representative in the Centennial Cup playoffs. The Sockeyes went undefeated en route to claiming the B.C. Junior Hockey League championship. No PCJHL club has ever won a single game in these playoffs. Coupled with the fact the Sockeyes went undefeated in the BCJHL playoffs, the Millionaires have reverted to their traditional role of underdogs. *We have absolutely nothing to lose and everything to gain whereas they have everything to lose,' says Tom Marsh, the coach who engineered Quesnel's first ever PCJHL title as a Junior A club. Marsh watched Richmond wrap up the BCJHL title in the fifth and deciding game of its best of nine championship series with Kelowna Packers. The 26- year-old bench boss thinks he has figured out a way to reel in the Sockeyes.

HUMBOLDT - When Bernie Lynch and his Humboldt Bronco coaching staff were assembling their roster for this season, they had one type of player in mind: a Duncan Ryhorchuk style-player. "Let's see, what are all the cliches - grinder, mucker, lunchpail type player?" said Lynch, the Broncos' coach, looking for the proper description of his scrappy forward. "Let me put it this way, he's a good, honest hockey player. If he comes by a pass, or comes by a goal, you know he came by it the hard way. He's the kind of guy that gives you callouses on your hands from patting him on the back.' With the Broncos hanging on to a 3-2 lead in the third period, Ryhorchuk tipped a point shot from Rob Rice past Pembroke netminder Grant Robb, giving Humboldt breathing space ahead of the hard-charging Pembroke team. With the 4-2 victory and a 7-2 win over Dartmouth Fuel Kids on Saturday, the Broncos assured themselves a spot in the playoff round, which begins Thursday. In Sunday's afternoon game, Dartmouth was beaten 7-3 by Richmond Sockeyes. Against Pembroke, the Broncos opened a 3-1 first-period lead on goals by Rice, Kevin Luke and Curtis Chamberlin, but struggled through the second period. The Lumber Kings dominated the play and pulled to within one on a power-play goal by Mike Eastwood, who scored both Pembroke goals. "We were just going through the motions in the second," said Ryhorchuk, who was selected Humboldt's player of the game. "You can't do that against these teams. We were able to play like that in our league, but all the teams here are champions. If you get a two-goal lead on them, they're not going to die."

There was a rude welcoming committee awaiting the Dartmouth Fuel Kids at the Centennial Cup tournament. After a trip which lasted close to 20 hours on Friday, Humboldt Broncos gave the Fuel Kids a 7-2 butt-kicking Saturday evening. Sunday afternoon, Dartmouth turned the other cheek, just to have it booted 7-3 by Richmond Sockeyes.

(He has also served as a trainer for the Vancouver Whitecaps, Nanaimo Clippers Junior A Tier 2 hockey team, this year's Centennial Cup Champion Richmond Sockeyes, last year's B.C. Winter Games, and the Mac's Super Series hockey tournament for the last three years; he was also the head trainer at UBC last year). Moro said it should simply be recognized that people with conventional first aid training have limitations when i it comes to sport-related injuries. He offered as an example a soccer tournament where both regular first aid attendents and sports aiders were on duty. During the course of a match, a player went down with a twisted ankle and was attended to by both "aiders." The conventional first aid attendent recommended splinting the leg and sending the player to emergency. But the sports aider iced the injury and taped the ankle for support and the player went back into the match a little later. Ice is a key ingredient to immediate and long-term treatment of most sports-related injuries as part of the "RICE" system. That is, rest, ice, compression and elevation. Rest simply means putting the injured part at rest while ice and elevation are used together for the same purpose. Icing the injured area constricts the flow of blood and, thus, decreases the injury bleeding into the area around it. And elevating the injury similarly slows blood flow. Richmond Sockeyes, champions of the B.C. Junior Hockey League, are now Western Canadian Tier Two Junior A champions. Wednesday night in Richmond, the Sockeyes defeated Humboldt Broncos 5-4 in the seventh and deciding game of the Abbott Cup. There was a controversial ending to the series as the Broncos failed to line up for the traditional series ending handshake - probably because, to the Broncos, the series isn't over. And that's | because the two teams will run into each other again as both were already assured berths in the four-team Centennial Cup tournament, the national Tier Two Junior A championship, which begins tonight (Saturday) in Humboldt. Richmond was given a berth in the Centennial Cup tournament because there were two spots reserved for Western teams, and Humboldt already had one as host team. Other teams in the tournament will be from Pembroke, Ont., and Dartmouth, Nova Scotia. Richmond took to the ice Tuesday holding a 3-2 lead in the series, but dropped a 4-3 overtime decision to force the seventh game. The first three games of the series were played in Humboldt, with Richmond winning two of them. Humboldt evened the series with a 2-1 victory Friday night, before Richmond scored a 5-3 win Sunday night to take the series lead. Richmond now has won playoff series this spring against Nanaimo Clippers, Delta Flyers, Kelowna Packers (for the BCJHL championship), Red Deer Rustlers (for the B.C. - Alberta championship) and Humboldt. Richmond and Humboldt are favored to meet in the Centennial Cup final next weekend. Today's opening game will pit the Broncos against the Fuel Kids from Dartmouth, and tomorrow (Sunday) the Sockeyes will take on the Lumber Kings from Pembroke. membership. Sometimes, in pursuit of truth, party lines get blurred. Ice thrills An early week bouquet is due the Red Deer Rustlers. The Rustlers are not the best team in the West • the Richmond Sockeyes and Humboldt Broncos will fight for that honor. But Red Deer's reborn Alberta Junior Hockey League team might be the best loved. They earned the community's support by overcoming seemingly insurmountable odds, by playing entertaining hockey, and by being more than a little brash. (The fans, mistakenly, thought throwing trash would show that they, too, were brash - but that's another story.) The Rustlers and their fans proved a couple of points: junior hockey can still draw here and a team can be operated successfully on a tight budget, without dragging down investors. They kept this town enthralled for a wild month of playoff hockey. And they may just have sealed their own fate, by showing once and for all that Red Deer could support a major junior hockey franchise if given the chance. Other voices Never underestimate the staying power of a bad idea, especially one that serves a political cause. Take "comparable worth" - or pay equity, as it has become known the belief that jobs can be objectively judged for their social value. The drive to establish pay equity by governmental action has slipped from the front pages lately, but its supporters have hardly abandoned their fight. The latest sign of the movement's vitality is a report by the National Committee on Pay Equity, whose board of directors includes such venerable organizations as the League of Women Voters, the American Library Association and the YWCA. Funded by the Ford Foundation, the report claims to be the first to confirm "what has long been suspected by pay equity advocates: that race, ethnicity and sex are all significant factors in setting pay." Actually, the report proves no such thing. It merely documents a well known fact: Women and minorities tend to be concentrated in certain jobs that tend to pay less than other jobs. The report's authors believe this pattern clinches the case for widespread discrimination. If such bias is so widespread, they suggest, what's the alternative to wholesale adjustment of wage scales? As imperfect as the present system may be in allocating income, it is far more responsive than the bureaucratic solution favored by the pay-equity forces. - An editorial from Rocky Mountain News, Denver.

In the other games, Kelowna Packers crushed Abbotsford Falcons 10-1, Penticton Knights downed Summerland Buckaroos 6.4 and Vernon Lakers thrashed 1 Merritt Warriors 13-4. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. Romeo was named most valuable player in the four-team, roundrobin series. British Columbia's Sockeyes skated to period leads of 2-0 and 2-1 before i Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third. Curtis Chamberlin and Kevin Luke scored for the Saskatchewan champion Broncos. 000 SHERBROOKE Jody Gage notched two goals Saturday to lift the Rochester Americans to a 43 comeback win over the Sherbrooke Canadiens in the second game of the American Hockey League final. Humboldt got spectacular netminding from Bruce Hoffort, who made 42 saves. Punch resigns Bruins' post NEW WESTMINSTER, B.C. (CP) - Ernie (Punch) McLean has resigned as head coach of the New Westminster Bruins of the Western Hockey League. McLean needs to spend more time with his mining interests in northern British Columbia, general manager John Olver said, adding McLean will remain with the club as a director. Olver said no decision has been made on a replacement. McLean took the Bruins to four consecutive Memorial Cup tournaments starting in 1975 and New Westminster won the Canadian title in 1977 and 1978. After selling his interest in the WHL team five years ago, McLean returned as coach in November, 1985. New Westminster finished fifth in the Western Division this season and missed the playoffs.

Fighting fans threaten series between juniors RED DEER, Alta. (CP) - The junior hockey series between the Red Deer Rustlers and the Richmond Sockeyes could be cancelled fans don't behave, the Canadian Amateur Hockey Association says. The CAHA said in a statement yesterday that unruly behavior of fans in both cities could result in the series being cancelled or moved to another city. One man was arrested during Sunday's game here, won 7-1 by the Rustlers. The best-of-seven between the Alberta and British Columbia junior champions is tied 2-2. paragraphs.text: Hockey degraded On Sunday, April 12, I attended the Rustlers vs Richmond Sockeyes gäme and was disappointed in the fans, refereeing and coaching. First, the fans were throwing garbage on the ice, raw fish for example, which delayed the game. I thought Red Deer citizens had more respect for the other team. Second, the coaches appeared to have lost control of their players. In the second period there were about six to eight fights, which shows the unsportsmanship of both teams. On the other hand, in the sport of football, if a player is fighting he IS out of the game immediately. I suggest that a player who is involved fighting should be suspended. Third. the coaches must begin to implement more positive player attitudes to insure that life injuries do not occur. Injuries may ruin the futures of great lavers. The true sport of hockey is only being degraded by that style of play on Sunday. J.K. Miller Red Deer

In the case of the Red Deer Rustlers, the disappointment was all too real and a touch bitter after dropping the Alberta-B.C. junior hockey playoff series to the Richmond Sockeyes Thursday. Placed in proper context, however, Red Deer's season must be considered a winner. Not only for the team but for junior hockey here. Crowds were always around 1,700 or better in the final 11 home playoff games which allowed management to balance the ledger. On the ice the Rustlers showed junior hockey has a place here. Off the ice the vast majority of fans showed they want it. The less said about the clowns, who nearly had the series cancelled after game four, the better. The true fans, to a large extent, helped the club. Their support was applauded by management. Their support I'm sure, spurred the club on when the going was rough or a series hung in the balance. And how many of those do we remember so well? The Rustlers pulled two stunning upsets by knocking off the favored Calgary Canucks in seven games and the St. Albert Saints in six to win the Alberta championship.

The mind was willing, but the legs wouldn't cooperate. Red Deer Rustlers, playing their 27th post-season game in 47 days, ran out of gas Thursday at the Arena. They were no match for the Richmond Sockeyes, who claimed a convincing 9-1 victory in the seventh and deciding game of the western Centennial Cup tier two junior hockey semifinal series. "There were too many (playoff) games in too few nights. It was a gruelling schedule and it took its toll," Rustler coach Larry Billows said sadly. "'The guys wanted it, but there was no intensity tonight. They just had nothing left." The Sockeyes, bigger and more talented than the Alberta Junior Hockey League champion Rustlers, blew open a scoreless game with five unanswered second-period goals. From there, it was simply a matter of if, and when, Red Deer would ruin goaltender Jamie Stewart's shutout. Pete deGraaf notched the lone Red Deer goal at 13:14 of the third period, deflecting Greg Pulliam's point shot into the Richmond goal with the Rustlers on a power play. The single goal was small consolation for the Rustlers, who dropped the first two games in Richmond, rallied to win three in a row at the Arena, then lost the final two. "I thought we were the better club the last three games," said a relaxed Orland Kurtenbach, the Sockeyes' general managercoach. "Certainly we deserved to win the last two, and the (7-5 Red Deer win) overtime game (Tuesday) could have gone either way. "I'm proud of these guys. They worked extremely hard." Equally as proud of his charges was Billows, who moved behind the Red Deer bench Feb. 4, just one month before the playoffs started. "I'm very happy with the way these guys played for me," he stressed. "I've never coached a team with so much character. There's a lot of heart in this dressing room." Rustler captain Pulliam agreed. "I'm just proud to be a part of this team," said the 20-year-old defenceman, who played the best hockey of his career along the playoff trail. "This team never quit, we never saw the end coming. "It seemed that there was always a light at the end of the tunnel. Even in the third period tonight, the light was still there." But it flickered, and eventually was snuffed out by the British Columbia champion Sockeyes, who got three goals from Dave Tomlinson and singles from Rob Clarke, Mike Claringbull, Jason Phillips, Jim Gunn,

By MONTE STEWART Herald-Tribune staff QUESNEL - Tom Marsh paused momentarily, looked around the dressing room and, eyeing Millionaires' netminder Scooter Henson, asked rhetorically: "Hey, Scoot, it is a little different, eh?" The Quesnel Millionaires had just defeated Grande Prairie North Stars 4-2 in the decisive game of the Peace Cariboo Junior Hockey League championship series Saturday night. The victory before a packed house at the Civic Arena culminated a rags to riches story for the Millionaires, who only a year ago were the doormats of the PCJHL. The Millionaires clinched the best-of-seven PCJHL championship series in five games. Friday, they thrashed the North Stars 8-2, setting the stage for Saturday's finale. "These guys have worked so hard all year long," said Marsh, the 26-year-old coach who, in his first full season of coaching at any level, lifted a team that won only six games last year out of the gutter and into the caviar set. Quesnel will now face B.C. Junior Hockey League champion Richmond Sockeyes in first round Centennial Cup play. Although Joe Dragon knocked in Murray Toews' goalmouth pass with 5:02 left in the third period to narrow the North Stars' deficit to 4-2 in the fightfilled clincher. But, for all intents and purposes, the game was over before the end of the middle frame. The Millionaires broke open a thrifty first period with three unanswered second- period markers. Don Doucette, Marty Peters and Warren Wurzer staked Quesnel to a 4-1 lead that the North Stars, try as they may, could not overcome. "We didn't have what it took to win the whole cake," said Grande Prairie captain Toews, whose assist extended his consecutive points streak to 61 games, regular season and playoffs combined. "'They had 21 guys that wanted to win that Peace Cariboo title." The contest was marred by a brawl with 6:14 remaining in the second period. The North Stars -

Maybe we shouldn't have traded him. I opined in this space a few weeks back that Langley Eagles coach and g.m. Muzz MacPherson made a wise move when he traded scoring ace Bryan Moller to the Richmond Sockeyes. But trust a sports journalist to be proved wrong. Seems Bryan (not Byron as quoted in one Canadian Press wire story recently) has been contributing greatly to the Junior A Sockeyes climb to glory in amateur hockey ranks. On Monday, Moller scored a goal in Richmond's 5-3 losing cause to the Humboldt Broncos in the first game of the best-ofseven Centennial Cup final to decide the Western Canadian champion. Though Richmond lost, Moller has been consistently good and may help, the 'Eyes turnaround this first spell of bad luck in an otherwise sparkling season. Game two in the series took place yesterday night (Tuesday) but passed press deadline.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - The Red Deer Rustlers paid a heavy price for penalties Wednesday night as the Richmond Sockeyes fired four powerplay goals in the third period for a 6-4 Centennial Cup playoff victory. However, the comeback victory that gave Richmond a 2-0 lead in the best-of-seven Western semifinal was spoiled by a post -game incident involving a thrown stick and a Red Deer player who stormed into the stands. The third and fourth games in the rough Tier II junior hockey series will be played Saturday and Sunday in Red Deer, Alta. An elderly woman was struck by a Red Deer stick thrown into the crowd. She received a cut and medical aid while the Rustlers' Brad Wilson took a match penalty. Teammate Rick Anderson, who climbed over the glass behind the Red Deer bench and nearly came to blows with a fan, received a gross misconduct. Red Deer coach Larry Billows, who attempted to intercede between Anderson and the fan, said spectators spat on his team and there was not enough security in the Richmond Arena. The Alberta coach had no real explanation for his team's collapse.

by Alan Watson It's getting to be that time for the Abbotsford Falcons. Thanks to the ¿Juan de Fuca Whalers, who enjoyed a four-win-one-loss week - one of those victories coming at the expense of the Falcons Abbotsford now find themselves looking quite a ways up to the the fourth and final playoff position in the B.C. Junior Hockey League's Coastal Division. As of Saturday, Nov. 29, the Falcons were only four points back of the Whalers. As of Sunday, Dec. 6, Abbotsford were 10 points behind the Whalers, who still have three games in hand. Falcons have lost a number of onegoal games this season, and were victimized again Friday night in Nanaimo, when the Whalers escaped with an 8-7 triumph in overtime. Saturday at MSA Arena, the Falcons controlled the final two periods against the second -place Richmond Sockeyes, received a sensational goaltending performance from rookie John Bukowski and took an exciting 3-2 win to keep their playoff hopes -alives. Abbotsford have 21 games left in the regular-season. Tuesday night at MSA Arena, the Whalers played a disciplined road game and skated away with a crucial 4-2 victory. Falcon coach Dave Cherniwchan was in a jovial mood following the big win over Richmond, the Falcons' first taste of victory against the defending Centennial Cup champions this season.

After being burned in a couple of B.C. Junior Hockey League deals this season, Abbotsford Falcon general manager Gary McCaskill is moving slowly towards finalizing a deal with the Coastal Division-leading Delta Flyers. The transaction is part-way completed, yet may not be finalized, McCaskill said on Monday. "There is a tentative deal in the works that could see Damien Holland, become an Abbotsford Falcon," said McCaskill. Holland, one of the premier goaltenders in the league, was in Abbotsford Saturday before the Falcons took to the ice against Richmond Sockeyes, and was ready to play, but McCaskill and Falcon owner Byron Hitchens decided at the last minute to hold off for a while before signing him to a playing card. "I got cold feet on the deal and I really can't say I know why," said McCaskill. "'We decided at the last minute not to card him. It was just a gut feeling. "I just got burned on the Shawn Crowthers' deal, and we've lost other guys we've traded for (Paul Thompson, Brent Jones). I pick up a couple players, and bang, they're gone. I want anybody we deal for to come in and want to be an Abbotsford Falcon. I didn't get that feeling from L Damien Holland right away. But, he's coming to practice and we'll have to see." If the deal with Delta for Holland goes through, it could involve as many as three Flyers coming to Abbotsford in exchange for one Falcon, that one obviously having to be a front-line player. HUMBOLDT, Sask. - A psychological ploy used by Humboldt Broncos' coach Bernie Lynch has blown up in his face. The 33-year-old Lynch, in his fourth season with Broncos, unwittingly offended the entire community here when he ordered his players not to shake hands with Richmond Sockeyes following the Sockeyes' western final triumph last Wednesday. Lynch figured that since the Broncos and Sockeyes would be meeting again in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey tournament, they would save the hand-shaking for this week. "Richmond ain't won nothing yet," said Lynch at the time. But Lynch, who grew up mainly in southern Ontario, badly misjudged the sensibilities of the people in this farming community. He has been under heavy criticism since the perceived breach in hockey etiquette last week and in Wednesday's edition of the Humboldt Journal, a weekly newspaper, Lynch was roasted in the letters to the editor section.

the beginning of a race and seeing it through to the finish line. And that difference can mean a great deal to an injured athlete. "The basis of first aid is to do the first treatment and then get them off to a doctor and that's the end of your commitment. "But in sports. first aid, our commitment is to get to the person, do the first aid, and look towards putting the person back on the field as quickly as possible," explained Greg Moro, a 17-year veteran of sports first aid who is one of the instructors for the Sport Medicine Council of B.C.'s SportsAid program. *We look at the long term rather than the short term," he continued. "*A normal first aid person gets a person for a short period of time and they never see them again, where we have a person and we see them all the way through their recovery." Part of following an athlete's recovery is the sports aider recognizing his limitations and referring the injured person to the hospital, family doctor or physiotherapist. Moro added, "Sports first aiders know a bit more about how the body reacts to injury so we . . . know what to do along with the icing (of certain injuries) so that healing takes place. "I think we look at the overall picture where the first aid person just looks at the start of the picture." None of this, however, is meant to criticize conventional first aid practices, stresses Moro, who has an industrial first aid "A ticket" and practices conventional and sports first aid at the Exhibition Park race track in Vancouver. (He has also served as a trainer for the Vancouver Whitecaps, Nanaimo Clippers Junior A Tier 2 hockey team, this year's Centennial Cup Champion Richmond Sockeyes, last year's B.C. Winter Games, and the Mac's Super Series hockey tournament for the last three years; he was also the head trainer at UBC last year.) Moro said it should simply be recognized that people with conventional first aid training have limitations when it comes to sport-related injuries. He offered as an example a soccer tournament where both regular first aid attendants and sports aiders were on duty. During the course of a match, a player went down with a twisted ankle and was attended by both "aiders." The conventional first aid attendant recommended splinting the leg and sending the player to emergency. But the sports aider iced the injury and taped the ankle for support and the player went back into the match a little later. Ice is a key ingredient to immediate and long-term treatment of most sports-related injuries as part of the "RICE" system. That is, 1 rest, ice, compression and elevation. Rest simply means putting the injured part at rest while ice and elevation are used together for the same purpose. Icing the injured area constricts the flow of blood and, thus, decreases the injury bleeding into the area around it. And elevating the injury

Junior hockey has come such a long way here in the past year, it would be a shame to lose its gains in one short, ugly series. The Red Deer Rustlers dropped out of the league last season, and came back determined to sell the game as an inexpensive, fun family outing. They succeeded, on and off the ice. After a halting start, the team responded to the direction of a new coach and is making a serious run for the Canadian title. Inexpensive season tickets filled the stands with vocal, loyal fans. But in the series against the Richmond Sockeyes, some of those "fans" have turned ugly. They have littered the ice after every Red Deer goal. They have pelted the Richmond players with eggs, and tried to fight them. These yahoos can scarcely be called fans. They are ruining the game and seriously impairing the Rustlers' chances of winning this series. The Canadian Amateur Hockey Association simply won't put up with these antics. The association is acutely aware of junior hockey's bad image, and has taken steps to improve it. Fighting has been reduced and stickwork curbed. The association can dictate what's unaceptable on the ice; it has less control over what goes on in the stands. If the louts in the Red Deer Arena persist, it has few options. It can forfeit the series to Richmond, cancel it completely, or move the remaining games to a neutral site. All three options are no-win propositions for the Rustlers. The Rustlers really need those games here. The players 1 need their fans behind them to help them defeat a more experienced team. And Rustler management needs the home-gate revenue to pay its bills and carry on. Once the game begins, Rustler security staff is almost powerless to control the impulsive actions of individual fans. But Rustler management can and must take actions before the game to limit the chances of another fiasco. Security staff can screen customers carefully as they enter the Arena, to ensure vegetables or other projectiles don't get in. Tight screening like this is routine at rock concerts and other sporting events. The true Rustler fans won't object to the inconvenience, because they know what is at stake, and condemn the yahoo minority responsible for the problem. Rustler management can also make an announcement before the game, outlining the consequences if the ice is littered, or Richmond players are pelted. After that, it's up to the fans themselves. If they want to see the Rustlers win this series, if they want junior hockey to endure and prosper in Red Deer, they have to discipline themselves.

After being cut, Hervey was considering calling it quits but after coming in contact with Orland Kurtenbach, a former National Hockey Leaguer with four teams over 11 seasons, Hervey's outlook on life changed for the better. Kurtenbach is the coach of the Richmond Sockeyes of the BCJHL. "Kurtenbach was a big influence on me. We had a talk about my off-ice attitude because I was letting a lot of little things bother me. "Playing under him really helped me out. He helped me make decisions,' continued Hervey. "'He said he'd get me a shot somewhere depending on how I played and we had a great year." In 17 regular season games, the hard-nosed blueliner had 24 points. The Sockeyes went on to win the Centennial Cup national tier 11 junior hockey championship at Humbolt. Sask., and Hervey was named to the first all-star team. "We had a great time. We had over ten, 20-year-olds and a lot of really good young guys with futures. We had five guys who had full rides to colleges back east," in the United States. Hervey, who couldn't stress enough that his favorite sport growing up was always hockey, pointed out that he prefers cool weather to the hot California sunshine and declared that to be successful, "it's real important to make hockey as fun as it can be and to have a really good work ethic." The 5', 11", 200-pounder, has brought his energetic a attitude to Moncton this season. He said he's, "still on cloud nine from the Winnipeg camp. (Jets' head coach) Dan Maloney was good to me and (general manager) John Ferguson was good to me." Hervey is presently on a 25- game amateur tryout and is waiting for a contract. He recently got word from the powers that be to get an apartment in Moncton, which means: a contract might be pending. He's been one of the steadiest defenceman for coach Rick Bowness this season, scoring one goal and assisting on two others and is second on the team in penalty minutes with 47. He's only played six games. "My game is to play physical, like clearing the front of •the net and going in the corners. I'm not a goon," he maintained. "And I'm an enforcer because I'm not a good enough fighter to be that...yet."

Red Deer Rustlers have a motto - 'Whatever it takes.' And that's what was required during the weekend as they posted back-to-back decisions over Richmond Sockeyes at the Arena, creating a 2-2 deadlock in the best-of-seven western Centennial Cup semifinal. Rustlers, with goaltender Ken Weiss turning in a magnificent performance, doubled the B.C. champions 6-3 Saturday, then zapped the visitors 7-1 Sunday in a chippy contest that featured 392 minutes in penalties, 221 to Richmond. The fifth game is Tuesday at 7:30 p.m. at the Arena. The weekend sweep was a complete turnaround from the first two games in Richmond, where Red Deer fell 11-4 and 6-4 and snapped a 17-game winning streak by the Sockeyes. "It took me one game to figure them out," smiled Rustler coach Larry Billows. "We had a couple of scouts tell us that Richmond would look strong the first game there, but that if we worked hard we could beat them in a best-of-seven series. "After watching them in Richmond, I had to agree. They're front-runners. I'm not predicting anything yet, but if they're going to win this series they're going to have to earn it. We're not going to let them waltz all the way to Humboldt (site of the 1987 Centennial Cup)." Sockeyes took the majority of the penalties Sunday out of frustration, based on bility to (1) solve netminder Weiss, and (2) manhandle the Rustlers. "We made good contact along the boards and in open ice," said Billows. "Richmond wasn't prepared for that. Maybe they don't hit like that in their league (BCJHL)." Brian Puhalsky, Tracy Georgsen and Rich Anderson - the later two on the power-play - staked Red Deer to a 3-0 lead and the hometown club, spurred on by 1,652 supporters, never looked back. Dixon Ward, Bob Duchak and Anderson doubled the Rustlers' margin in a wild second period that was delayed for 30 minutes by four on-ice battles, as well as a fracas behind the Richmond players bench that was instigated by a group of disorderly fans. Dave Tomlinson, with his fourth goal of the series, finally got the Sockeyes on the board at 1:39 of the third period, but Weiss shut the door the rest of the way.

By ELLIOTT PAP. Sun Sports Reporter HUMBOLDT, SASK. - Richmond Sockeyes finally received the "fair shake" from the officiating that coach Orland Kurtenbach had been praying for Wednesday night. Sadly for the Sockeyes, that's the only item which went their way in a key Centennial Cup game against Humboldt Broncos. Richmond was thrashed 6-1 by the host team as the round-robin portion of the national junior A hockey tournament ended. The loss means the 'Eyes have to come back tonight and face Ontario's Pembroke Lumber Kings in the semi-final game. Humboldt advances to Saturday's winner-take-all final. The Broncos were 3-0 in the round-robin, Richmond 2-1, Pembroke 1-2 and Atlantic champion Dartmouth 0-3. "We have no excuses," said Kurtenbach. "'The refereeing was good. We just got outhustled, outskated, out everythinged. But it's over and done with now. We can only use it as a motivating tool. "Some nights it just doesn't go, and tonight was one of them.' Humboldt's Bill McDougall and Richmond's Stan Czenezek traded first-period power-play goals before the Broncos broke things open in the second.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. - A psychological ploy used by Humboldt Broncos' coach Bernie Lynch has blown up in his face. The 33-year-old Lynch, in his fourth season with the Broncos, unwittingly offended the entire community here when he ordered his players not to shake hands with Richmond Sockeyes following the Sockeyes' western final triumph last Wednesday. Lynch figured that since the Broncos and Sockeyes would be meeting again in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey tournament, they would save the hand-shaking for this week. "Richmond ain't won nothing yet," said Lynch at the time. But Lynch, who grew up mainly in southern Ontario, badly misjudged the sensibilities of the people in this farming community. has been under heavy criticism since the perceived breach in hockey etiquette last week and in Wednesday's edition of the Humboldt Journal, a weekly newspaper, Lynch was roasted in the letters to the editor section

By ELLIOTT PAP The 'Eyes have it. Orland Kurtenbach's Richmond Sockeyes collected their first B.C. Junior Hockey League championship in eight years Tuesday night, edging Kelowna Packers 3-2 at Richmond Arena to sweep the bestof-nine final five straight. The Sockeyes, who are loaded with major junior veterans, made it look easy as they romped to 13 consecutive wins in three BCJHL series. "I must admit that is a surprise," said Kurtenbach, the former Vancouver Canuck captain and coach. "'You just never know what the fickle finger of fate will do. But we seemed to have it in gear. I'm very honored and very happy to be a winner my first year coaching in junior." The Sockeyes' season is far from over, however. As a tier two t team, their particular dream goes all the way to the Centennial Cup national championship.

(CP) - Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Saturday night with a 5-2 win over the Humboldt Broncos. But the Sockeyes had to fall back on an outstanding performance in goal by Frank Romeo, who stood up to 46 Humboldt shots while his teammates managed only 23 at the Broncos net. Romeo was named most valuable player in the four-team, roundrobin series. . British Columbia's Sockeyes skated to period leads of 2-0 and 2-1 before Paul Rutherford scored the winning goal in the third. paragraphs.text: An all-Spokane officiating crew will work the exhibition game between the U.S. and Canadian Olympic teams Nov. 28 in Tacoma. Dennis LaRue, the lone American selected to referee in the 1988 Olympics, will be the referee in the Tacoma Dome. His linesmen will be Kerry Blair and Cory Bowles. All three formerly played Junior B hockey in Spokane, and LaRue played one year of senior amateur. LaRue and Blair also will work the U.S.-Canada game Nov. 27 in Los Angeles. Mark Rudolph, officiating program director of the Amateur Hockey Association of the U.S., said LaRue may receive other exhibition assignments. -Don Cherry, who played pro hockey for the 1962-63 Spokane Comets, has been inducted into the Rochester (N.Y.) Americans Hall of Fame. Cherry played and coached for Rochester in the pro American Hockey League after leaving Spokane. He later coached in the National Hockey League. Joe Crozier, another former Comet defenseman, NHL coach and Rochester coach, was one of the e original inductees in the Americans' Hall of Fame last year. I Dan Holden, the only local player ever to play for the Spokane Chiefs in the Western Hockey League, is playing for Acadia University in Wolfville, Nova Scotia. Holden played at the University of Calgary in 1991-85, then joined the Chiefs the following season. Former Chief defenseman Rick Herbert, who holds the WHL record of 329 games played, has joined the Richmond Sockeyes of the Tier Two British Columbia Junior Hockey League. Marc Pezzin, who coached Herbert in Spokane and Brandon, said Herbert was not interested in playing a sixth season in the after being released by the Chicago Blackhawks. Herbert plans to play college hockey in Canada next year, Pezzin said.

The former Langley Eagles have finally landed at a new home - and it will be Chilliwack, not Richmond as first proposed. After hearing strenuous objections to their proposed move to Richmond, the B.C. Junior Hockey League team finalized a deal on Friday, June 12, that will see the club settle in the Fraser Valley City. Eagles had severed their longtime relationship with Langley last month, giving up their ice time at the Langley Civic Centre when no satisfactory practice times could be negotiated. But a proposal to move to Seafair Arena in Richmond met with stern disapproval from the rival Richmond Sockeyes, who play out of Richmond Arena. "We thought we could talk Richmond (Sockeyes) into setting up a rivalry, but they would have no part of it," said Eagles coach and general manager Buzz MacPherson. The new team, to be called the Chilliwack Eagles, has received approval from the league and Chilliwack Council. It will play out of Twin Rinks Arena in Sardis, one mile south of Chilliwack. "We regret moving out of Langley, but the Civic Centre couldn't accommodate us," said MacPherson. Prime icetime in the Chilliwack Coliseum has already been allocated to the still-un-named Chilliwack entry in the Pacific Northwest Hockey League. In other related news, Chilliwack's Dave Archibald, who scored 107 points for the Portland Winter Hawks of the Western Hockey League last season and was named the WHL's most sportsmanlike player, was drafted sixth overall in the National Hockey League's amateur draft by the Minnesota North Stars and Langley forward Neil Eisenhut1 was selected in the twelfth round by the Vancouver Canucks.

RICHMOND, B.C. (CP) - Mike Gaber scored at 3:31 of overtime Tuesday night to give the Humboldt Broncos a 4-3 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes and new life in their Centennial Cup playoff series. The victory forced a seventh and deciding game in the Western Canada final here Wednesday night. Bill McDougall led the Broncos with two goals and Curtis Chamberlin was the other marksman for Humboldt which trailed 1-0 at the end of the first period and was tied 2-2 entering the third period. Paul Rutherford led the Sockeyes with two goals while Dave Tomlinson added the other goal for Richmond which was outshot 46-45. Humboldt, which got spectacular netminding from Bruce Hoffort who made 42 saves, scored twice on the power play while Richmond counted once with the man advantage. There were two key mistakes Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach felt gave Humboldt the victory.

The win gave the British Columbia champions a 2-0 record in the roundrobin portion of the tournament, and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champions, Humboldt Broncos. Dave Tomlinson led Richmond with two goals, while Brian Kozak and Stan Czenczek scored one each. Czenczek scored the winning goal at 6:35 of the third period, after his team held a 1-0 lead through the first two frames.

By FRANK LUBA Sports Reporter For the 1986-87 Richmond Sockeyes, the Centennial Cup championship is now just a memory. For the bulk of the team, Richmond will soon be just a memory, too. Of this year's roster, only 16-year-olds Rob Sumner and Jason Talo appear set to return to the Sockeyes, who won B.C.'s second consecutive Canadian junior A championship by downing the Humboldt Broncos 5-2 Saturday. The nine 20-year-olds forming the experienced core of the team are no longer eligible. That includes standouts like goaltender Frank Romeo, the Centennial Cup's most valuable player, and defenceman Matt Hervey, who had a tryout with the Los Angeles Kings last year. Sockeyes' president Horst Wilkomm is thinking about taking a break from hockey and even coach Orland Kurtenbach must decide his future. Humboldt will also be the scene of mass departures. In addition to several players taking U.S. college scholarships, the Broncos will lose their seven 20-year-olds and maybe head coach Bernie Lynch. Lynch, also the Saskatchewan champions' general manager, raised the ire of Humboldt fans following the Broncos' refusal to shake hands with the Sockeyes when Richmond won the Western Canadian. championship. The incident provoked letters of outrage in the weekly Humboldt Journal and much muttering among the Uniplex crowds.

Sun News Services RED DEER, Alta. - Red Deer Rustlers beat the Richmond Sockeyes 7-5 in overtime Tuesday in a Centennial Cup playoff hockey game that saw no repeat of the unruly fan behavior that marred Sunday's match. The victory enabled the Rustlers to take a 3-2 lead in the best-of-seven tier two junior western semi-final series. The sixth game goes here tonight. "There wasn't a bit of trouble," said Sockeye coach Orland Kurtenbach. "The management asked the fans to refrain from throwing anything on the ice and there was no problem." Dixon Ward's goal 59 seconds into the 10-minute overtime period was the winner. Geoff Gosselin added an insurance marker at 9:50 into an empty net just after Sockeye Jason Phillips missed a penalty shot. The Sockyes were led by Brian Kozak, who had two goals, while Paul Rutherford, Mike Claringbull and Phillips contributed singles. The Rustlers held period leads of 3-2 and 4-3, fell behind 5-4 in the third period but managed to pull even at 13:16 to force the overtime. Richard Anderson and Brian Puhalsky each scored twice for Red Deer and Greg Pulliam added a single.

The banquet was as impressive as always when the federal government is treating. Ben Johnson, Todd Brooker and Carolyn Waldo, the cream of our athletes, were served up on the half shell along with someone named Doug Mitchell, who is not the commissioner of the Canadian Football League. Mitchell is a 32-year-old school teacher who was honored because he pulled his junior hockey team out of a playoff series in Ontario last month. For this he received the r Fair Play Award, warm words from sports minister Otto Jelinek and a standing ovation. When you hear of the Animal House ARCHIE behavior un- McDONALD dermining the junior series between Red Deer and Richmond Sockeyes, when you read of nine American League players being ejected because of a brawl in the warmup, when you see the Canadian and Soviet juniors end in a war, the Fair Play award is like hockey's Nobel Peace Prize. Mitchell is a hockey idealist. He played four years at University of Waterloo and then five years of Intermediate A. When pressed, he would get involved in fisticuffs because that is the way the game has been accepted. But when he thought about it, he concluded, "What the hell is fighting doing in hockey anyway? When you pose that question to yourself, it is ridiculous. Fighting is illegal, yet it is allowed to happen." MONTREAL (CP) - The 1987 Capitcher, Grand Prix race in Montreal is dead, the victim of a bitter brewery battle. Roger Dore of Labatt Breweries Ltd.® announced Monday that atAtlanta to hold the race, set for June 14, have failed. Labatt, sponsor of the event for the last nine years, fought Molson Breweries Ltd., which wanted to run the race, in the courts, accusing its riGiants of unethical behavior. The uncertainty prompted the Federation international du sport automobile to remove the race from the Formula One calendar last month. But Labatt insisted it could overcome the problems which it claimed were due to foul play on the part of Molson, promoter Jack Long, the City of Montreal and the Formula One Constructors Association, which controls the drivers and their sponsors. Admitting defeat for this year, Dore told reporters Labatt "will continue working to have the Grand Prix Labatt reinstated "for next year for years after that.' (CP) The Richmond Sockeyes scored four goals in the third period to beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night in the Centennial Cup Junior A hockey championship tournament. The win gave the British Columbia champions a 2-0 record in the roundrobin portion of the tournament, and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champions, Humboldt Broncos. It was the second straight loss for the Lumber Kings, Ontario's representatives in the four-team tournament.

(CP) The Richmond Sockeyes exploded for three goals in the third period to beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 Monday night in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship tournament. The win gave the British Columbia champions a 2-0 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament, and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos. It was the second straight loss for the Lumber Kings, the Ontario representatives in the fourteam tournament. The other team in the championship event, the Dartmouth Fuel Kids from British Columbia, are also at 0-2. The teams that finish second and third in the round-robin portion of the tournament meet in a semi-final game on Thursday night. The winner of the semifinal tangles with the team that finishes first after round-robin play. Dave Tomlinson led Richmond with two goals, while Brian Kozak and Stan Czenczek had one each. Czenczek scored the winning goal at 6:35 of the third period, after his team held a 1-0 lead through the first two frames. George Dupont scored for the Lumber Kings, who were outshot 26-25. The Sockeyes were assessed 11 of 15 minor penalties. By MONTE STEWART Herald-Tribune staff The acronym for the B.C. Junior Hockey League is BCJHL. It is an abbreviation which can now afford an extra "C' for r champion. Richmond Sockeyes rendered the Peace Cariboo Junior Hockey League still winless in Centennial Cup playoffs by sweeping Quesnel Millionaires in a best-of-three B.C. Tier II championship series last weekend. (If Grande Prairie North Stars had defeated Quesnel in the PCJHL finals, they also would have played for the B.C. championship because the league is sanctioned by the B.C. Amateur Hockey Association.) The Sockeyes drubbed the Millionaires 5-1 in Richmond on Saturday night. Friday, the hosts edged the PCJHL champion Quesnel 4-3. Richmond now advances to meet Red Deer Rustlers in the next round of the Centennial Cup playoffs. The Rustlers locked up the Alberta Junior Hockey League title with an 8-5 triumph over St. Albert Saints on Friday night. Red Deer took the best-of-seven AJHL final 4-2. With the victories over the Millionaires, the Sockeyes remained undefeated in the playoffs. Their post-season mark now stands at 16-0. The BCJHL-AJHL showdown will be a best-of-seven affair. The first two games will be held in Richmond and the remaining games - depending on how many are necessary - will be held in Red Deer. Although the Mills came close Friday, the PCJHL champions haven't won a single game against BCJHL clubs in Centennial Cup playoffs. Penticton Knights blanked Prince George Spruce Kings 2-0 in last year's best-of-three series and went on to win the Centennial Cup.

HUMBOLDT, Sask. (CP) - Jason Phillips scored three goals to lead the Richmond Sockeyes to an easy 7- 3 win over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship Sunday. It was the second loss in two days for Dartmouth. The Nova Scotians opened the week-long, round-robin tournament Saturday with a 7-2 defeat at the hands of the host Humboldt Broncos. Bryon Moller, Steve Jaques, Dave Tomlinson and Matt Hervey also scored for the Sockeyes, who piled up 44 shots against Dartmouth's 29. Richmond zipped to a 3-1 lead in the first period and built the margin to 6- 2 in the second.

by Larry Pruner Suddenly, the Richmond Sockeyes are playing darn good hockey. And on Wednesday night in Richmond, they did it at the Delta Flyers' expense. The defending Centennial Cup champions broke open a 2-2 tie in the second period with five unanswered goals and breezed to a 7-2 thrashing of the Flyers in B.C. Junior Hockey League play. The loss dropped the Flyers' first-place record to 23-6 on the season, while the Sockeyes moved into second spot 11 points behind the coastal division leaders. "They beat us handily," conceded Flyers' general manager and coach Rick Hopper. "They have a whole new line-up. There's not going to be another easy game against the Richmond Sockeyes."

After a valiant season; struggling from behind time after time to eventually win the Peace- Cariboo Junior Hockey League title, the Quesnel Millionaire's fell short in their bid for the Mowat Cup, the pinnacle of success in B.C. Tier II Junior hockey, to the Richmond Sockeyes.

Clippers scored at the 17-second mark of sudden death overtime to trim the Falcons 4-3 in B.C. Junior Hockey League action at MSA Arena. Falcons host Richmond Sockeyes tonight (Saturday) at 8 p.m.

RED DEER. Alta. - The Richmond Sockeyes scored five first-period goals and coasted to a 7-3 victory over the Red Deer Rustlers to square their western Centennial Cup semifinal at three wins each last night. The Sockeyes, British Columbia Junior Hockey League champions, led 5-1 after the first period and 5-3 after the second. The Sockeyes outshot the Rustlers, Alberta Junior Hockey League champions, 36-33. Rob Clarke scored three times for Richmond and Tony Bobbitt, Dave Tomlinson, Brian Kozak and Mike McCormick added single goals. Pete DeGraaf with two goals and Dixon Ward replied for Red Deer. The Rustlers took 18 of 31 minor penalties and two misconducts. The deciding game in the series will be played tonight in Red Deer.
