RICHMOND, B.C. Any hockey team that wins 17 consecutive post-season games, a feat Richmond Sockeyes have now accomplished, requires more than its share of breaks. Sockeyes got a large dose of good fortune with 13 minutes remaining in Wednesday's Western Canada Centennial Cup semifinal game against Red Deer Rustlers at the Richmond Arena. Trailing 4-3, Richmond was handed a five-minute power play when Rustler defenceman Keith Lightbown was ejected for pummelling Sockeye forward Dave Tomlinson, who never removed his gloves. To that point, the Rustlers had used the splendid goaltending of pick-up Ken Weiss and a disciplined defence to keep the explosive Sockeyes at bay. It was far and away a superior effort to the one they turned in Tuesday, when they were hammered 11-4 in the opener of the best-of-seven set. But killing a five-minute penalty without the Sockeyes inflicting massive damage was simply too much to ask. Richmond scored three goals while Lightbown was removing his equipment and pulled out a 6-4 victory. Sockeyes now lead the series 2-0, but will have to win it in Red Deer, where the remainder of the games will be played. The third game is Saturday at 8 p.m. and the fourth Sunday at 7 p.m. "We had a game plan and stuck to it for two periods, but then we had a little misfortune with the referee and a little misfortune with one of our players," said an angry Rustler coach Larry Billows. "That was a dumb play by Lightbown. I don't know what he was thinking of." Dean Rutledge, who started the season with Fort McMurray Oil Barons, drew the Sockeyes even at 9:52, defenceman Matt Hervey notched the winner his third of the game at 11:19, and Brian Kozak added some insurance 88 seconds later. "We took some dumb penalties tonight as well, so I guess they evened out," offered Richmond general manager-coach Orland Kurtenbach, who stressed his club had to work extra hard to pull out the victory. "It didn't come easy for us," he stated. "We had 36 shots in the first two periods and weren't getting the puck past their goaltender (Weiss). S-P Services It was a series decided as much by timely bounces as tight checking, and the Richmond Sockeyes got the last bounce at the right time. Richmond's Dave Tomlinson scored with 2:37 remaining in regulation time Wednesday night to snap a 4-4 tie and give Richmond a 5-4 victory visiting Humboldt Broncos to thin the Abbott Cup. Sockeyes won the bestof-seven Western .Canada Tier Two junior hockey series four games to three. Richmond and Humboldt each won two one-goal games in a series that saw the Sockeyes outscore the Broncos 26-24. "Our guys played super hockey here," said Humboldt coach Bernie Lynch. "I can't ask them to play better than they did. But the way the puck seemed to find a way to jump over our sticks, it just seemed that somebody up there said, 'Sorry, but you're not going to win tonight.' "I told them after the game to remember that this wasn't a bestof-seven, it's a best-of-nine. If we' beat them in the Centennial Cup, nobody will remember who the Western champion was."

There are two things the Humboldt Broncos do very consistently: one is win hockey games, the other is make money. Although they failed to accom-' plish the former on Wednesday, losing 5-4 to the Richmond Sockeyes in the seventh game of their Abbott Cup series in Richmond, they almost certainly will do the latter with the Centennial Cup tournament which officially opens in Humboldt on Friday. The Broncos, who turned smalltime hockey drafts into a bigleague operation, have taken the Centennial Cup tournament and turned it into another winner. With Cup tickets going as an eight-game package for $50, the team managed t to virtually sell out the 2,400-seat Uniplex. Add to that the contributions of eight or nine sponsors, including the Bauer skate company, and the Broncos were looking at adding to their already impressive bank account. by Russ Ullyot An old shootout got the B.C. Junior Hockey League season started at the Chilliwack Coliseum on Friday. But when the smoke cleared, the Chilliwack Eagles fluttered to the ground and the visiting Richmond Sockeyes escaped town with an 8-6 victory. The opening game in the 1987-88 BCJHL season saw two teams of opposite character go at each other. The Sockeyes' skaters had the upper hand over the grinding style of the Eagles. On the bright side, Darryl Anderson notched a hat-trick for the Eagles with other markers coming off the sticks of Greg Anderson, Mike Dutton and Cam Matches. For Richmond, Brian McFarlane had a five-point night, scoring twice and adding three assists. Mario Desjardins notched two goals, with singles to Scott Miller, John Atkinson and Tony Bobbitt. Richmond outshot Chilliwack 43-25. The Sockeyes were decididly out-hit by the Eagles. But Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach countered, "'We outshot them." Kurtenbach added, "It's not the kind of game you like to see, and you only hope you're on the right end of the score at the end." The Eagles held a 4-3 lead after the first 20 minutes. Darryl Anderson opened the scoring on a three-way passing play from Jim Butterworth and Derby Bognar that gave him a wide open net in which to slip the puck. Richmond scored the only two second-period markers to take a 5-4 lead, and the Sockeyes added a power play marker and a short-handed goal in the third en route to the win. The Eagles were held to two power play opportunities, while giving the Sockeyes three chances with the man advantage. Quill Notes Chilliwack minor hockey was well represented in the opening-day lineup for the Eagles. In uniform for Chilliwack were Bart McAstocker, Jerry Lund, Bryan Padgham and Marc Gaultier. Padgham and Gaultier each picked up one assist in the contest. Padgham also had three minor penalties, while Gaultier handled himself well in the game's only fight late in the third period. Unfortunately for Lund, the game ended abruptly midway through the third period when he went down in a collision at centre ice. Lund will be out two weeks with strained knee ligaments. The Eagles' next games are on home ice Friday and Saturday (Sept. 25 and 26). On Friday, the Eagles will play at the Coliseum against the Summerland Buckaroos before shifting to Vedder Twin Rinks on Saturday to host the Abbotsford Falcons.

NOTEBOOK: OK: Brophy rewarded From The Whig- Standard wire services A year ago, coach Dan Maloney took the underdog Toronto Maple Leafs to the deciding game of the Norris Division final. Leaf owner Harold Ballard was initially impressed by his team's performance, but then offered his coach a new contract without a substantial raise. Maloney considered the incident an insult, so he left Toronto and signed on to coach the Winnipeg Jets. This year, new Toronto coach John Brophy once again brought the Leafs to within one game of the National Hockey League semifinal playoffs. And this time, just two days after the Leafs were eliminated by the Detroit Red Wings, Ballard gave his man a new three-year contract with what Brophy described as a "substantial" raise. Assistant coach Garry Lariviere of Gananoque also got a new three-year pact. 0 Pembroke Lumber Kings clinched a spot in the semifinal game of the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship tournament last night with an 8-4 victory over the Dartmouth Fuel Kids. The Ontario champions will meet the loser of today's final round-robin game between Humboldt Broncos and Richmond Sockeyes, who both have 2-0 records. O Longueuil Chevaliers scored three goals in 47 seconds in the first period en route to a 10-4 triumph over Chicoutimi Sagueneens to win the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League championship last night. The Chevaliers, who won the best-of-seven series 4-1, will represent Quebec at the Memorial Cup tournament in Oshawa, which opens on Saturday.

by Alan Watson So close and yet . . . back 'to eight points out of a playoff position. A week in the B.C. Junior Hockey League which began so promising for the Abbotsford Falcons ended Sunday with the team back to realizing making up for a 1-9-1 start means only one thing hard work, and lots of it. Like, for the remaining 29 games of the regular season. Last Tuesday, the Falcons looked great in a 7-3 thumping of the Chilliwack Eagles. Saturday night at MSA Arena, they zoomed to a 3-1 first period lead against the second-place Richmond Sockeyes, but mental mistakes and costly turnovers resulted in an 8-4 setback. And Sunday in Juan de Fuca, against the Whalers who hold down the fourth and final playoff position in the Coastal Division, a tired Abbotsford team fell behind 6-2, battled back to 7-6 and lost it 8-6 after giving up an empty net goal. "We were enthused coming off the road and really pleased with our defensive game," said coach Jim Frost. "It seemed to carry over into the Chilliwack game and we played really well. It started us off well against Richmond, we made those mental mistakes and it hurt us. 1 It is disappointing." It didn't help matters that the Whalers went out and won three of four games to give themselves some breathing room, nor did it help matters that Sunday's game on the Island was Abbotsford's ninth in 17 days. However, Frost said the game plan is to carry on as usual. The team has improved drastically in the past month, and as Frost noted, "'We are so gol' dang close!" Against Richmond, the Sockeyes scored their first seven goals either shorthanded or on the powerplay, and Richmond coach Orland Kurtenback was breathing a little easier after watching his team snap a four-game losing streak. "We haven't been getting very good hockey out of our players," noted Kurtenbach. "We've been taking dumb penalties and getting ourselves in trouble. Tonight we got the individual efforts a team needs, which we hadn't been getting. We also played with a little cohesion, moved the puck and got some goals out of it."

BRONCOS Mike Gaber RICHMOND, SERIES| overtime Tuesday night to give the Humboldt Broncos a 4-3 victory over the Richmond Sockeyes and new life in their Centennial Cup playoff series. The victory forced a seventh and deciding game in the Western Canada final here Wednesday night. Bill McDougall led the Broncos with two goals and Curtis Chamberlain was the other marksman for Humboldt, which trailed 1-0 at the end of the first period and was tied 2-2 entering the third Paul led the periderford Sockeyes with two goals while Dave Tomlinson added the other goal for Richmond which was outshot 46-45. Humboldt, which got spectacular netminding from Bruce Hoffort who made 42 saves, scored twice on the power play while Richmond counted once with the man advantage. There were two key mistakes Richmond coach Orland Kurtenbach felt gave Humboldt the victory. The first occurred seconds after Tomlinson scored to put the Sockeyes ahead 3-2 at 7:44 of the third period. Tony Bobbitt took a penalty for closing his hand on the puck and McDougall tied the game on the power play. The second incident came early in the 10-minute full-time overtime when Steve Jaques took a five-minute match penalty for -butting McDougall behind the play. McDougall picked up a holding minor on the play but the Broncos scored while the teams were playing even strength and then just kept the pressure on the Sockeyes with their power play. Jaques who was with the Broncos earlier this year faces at least a one suspension. •In Medicine Hat, Alta., Brian Gerrits scored at 15:23 of the first overtime period to lead the Portland Winter Hawks to a 3-2 win over the Medicine Hat Tigers in the second game of the Western Hockey League final.

... It's nice to see Nanaimo product Al Hill alive and well with Philadelphia Flyers in the playoffs. A lot of younger, people I've been talking sports with have never heard of Hill. He celebrated his 32nd birthday in game three by scoring the winner in Philly's 4-1 win over the Islanders. He then added another goal in game four Sunday, a 6-4 Flyer victory. Hill, who spent most of the year with Hershey Bears of the American League, collected 47 goals and 123 points in two seasons (1974-76) with Victoria Cougars. Ignored by the NHL, but drafted in 1975 by Indianapolis Racers of the World Hockey Association, Hill signed with the Flyers as a free agent and his best year with Philly came in 1979-80 when he counted 16 goals in 61 games. His biggest fan locally is his brother-in-law Jim Bayliss, who can be seen over the winter performing linseman duties at Clipper games. Greg Heakes of the Medicine Hat News tells me defenceman Wayne McBean is drawing high raves from NHL scouts as the June amateur lottery approaches. McBean has played both the powerplay and shorthanded situations as the Tigers have skated into the Western Hockey League finals against Portland Winter Hawks. David McNab, chief scout with Hartford Whalers, flew to Alberta in February for a private dinner meeting with McBean. "The idea of interviewing the kids is new," said Medicine Hat General Manager Russ Farwell. "They (scouts) are trying to judge a guy's character at 17. It is a shot in the dark for most teams.' McNab figures McBean will go in the top five picks, but Montreal Canadiens' Wilson wouldn't be as specific, saying McBean would be chosen in the first three rounds. HUMBOLDT, Sask./ The Canadian Press The Richmond Sockeyes exploded for four goals in the third period to beat Pembroke Lumber Kings 4-1 last night in the Centennial Cup junior A hockey championship tournament. The win gave the British Columbia champions a 2-0 record in the round-robin portion of the tournament, and a share of first place with the Saskatchewan champion Humboldt Broncos. Pembroke has a 0-2 record.
The payoff is a best-of-three series against the Richmond Sockeye's, champions of the B.C. Junior Hockey League.
